# engineer-of-source experience — Marix

## Workflow runtime

- The active hierarchy is Task → Intent → Step → Invocation/Relay. Intent owns plain Plan state; there is no active Plan actor or protocol namespace.
- Register every child actor in its task-wide registry before `start`. On termination, commit lifecycle result/status and complete the registry entry before notifying the owner; reversing either order creates lookup races.
- `Lifecycle` stores `ActorStatus<Result>` atomically. Parent updates carry the terminal child result; use registry result lookup only for dynamic reconstruction.
- Task uses a current-thread Tokio runtime driven by a dedicated standard thread. Spawn child actors through that shared runtime; blocking the task dispatcher prevents every nested actor from progressing.
- `TaskAccess` permits construction/insertion only. Keep lookup, completion, cancellation, lineage checks, and routing in `TaskRuntime`; retain its weak `SessionContext` link.

## Model and tool boundaries

- Deepseek is stream-only. Buffer SSE tool-call fragments by index, require a valid terminal `[DONE]`, validate complete JSON-object arguments, then emit one normalized `StepDraft`; never execute partial deltas.
- 2026-07-20: DeepSeek chat-completion payloads explicitly disable Thinking in the base payload because Thinking rejects `tool_choice`; keep forced tool relays on `tool_choice: "required"` and non-tool relays on JSON response format.
- Invocation validates the exact registered tool name and JSON arguments. Return validation and unknown-tool failures through normal typed results so parent actors cannot strand.
- Windows managed processes launch executables directly with explicit arguments. Validate UUID paths, reject symlinks, bind ownership to PID plus creation time, bound output pages, and retain the lock file.
- 2026-07-21: Native web search invokes DuckDuckGo, Yahoo, and Wikipedia through curl with a 60-second per-attempt `--max-time`; preserve provider fallback order and retry settings together.
- 2026-07-22: Tool-call-summarize is a native Workflow Tool call (`workflow_call_summary`, in `protocol/workflow/call_summary.rs`), not an isolated JSON-mode relay — `RelayKind::ToolCallSummarize` is a struct variant `{ invocation: InvocationSignature, tool: String, output: String }` (sibling to the unit variant `RelayKind::IntentAnalyze`), and `RelayRuntime::model_request`/`context_prompts` in `server/relay/prompt.rs` stay a *single unconditional* code path shared by both variants; only one extra trailing prompt string differs, appended via `if let RelayKind::ToolCallSummarize { tool, output, .. } = &self.kind { prompts.push(Self::tool_call_prompt(tool, output)); }` right after `pending_intent_prompt`. This keeps the system prompt + every context-chain prompt except the last byte-identical between a normal decision call and a summarize call for the same intent state — that prefix-identity (for model-provider prompt/token-cache reuse) is the entire point of the design, so any future change to this path must preserve it rather than re-forking per-`RelayKind` request builders.
- 2026-07-22: `IntentRuntime::verdict()` (`server/intent/runtime.rs`) and the analogous `InvocationRuntime::render_workflow_policy()` (`server/invocation/runtime.rs`) both hardcode the literal string `"WorkflowPolicy"` three times (in `Prompt::load(...)`, the panic-detail format, and two more error messages) rather than factoring it into a local `template` variable — unlike `RelayRuntime::system_prompt`'s style in `server/relay/prompt.rs`, which does use a local `let template = "System";`. When adding a third `Prompt::load` call site for the same template name, match whichever sibling call site is closer in shape/intent rather than assuming one style is universal across the codebase.
- 2026-07-21: A model backend can be fully self-contained (e.g. `src/server/model/glm/`): own its own `XConfig` struct inside `backend.rs` instead of `common/config`, take it directly in `new(config)`, and stay unreferenced except for the two mirrored lines in `model/mod.rs` (`pub mod x;` / `pub use x::XBackend;`). A `pub use`-exported but never-constructed backend does not trigger a dead-code warning under `cargo check`, so an intentionally-unwired sibling backend compiles clean without `#[allow(dead_code)]`. GLM's chat-completions API is OpenAI-compatible SSE, so its stream accumulator mirrors Deepseek's byte-for-byte (same five-tool-call cap, same `stop`/`tool_calls` finish_reason values, same `thinking: {"type":"disabled"}` payload field).
- 2026-07-21: Deepseek's and Glm's near-duplicate stream/request/payload logic was consolidated into `src/server/model/openai.rs` (`pub(super) struct OpenAiCore`, sibling-scoped so `deepseek`/`glm` submodules can use it but nothing outside `model` can). Each backend now holds `core: OpenAiCore` and delegates `ModelBackendImpl`/`Debug`. Provider-specific text (log/error strings) is threaded through a `provider: &'static str` field/param instead of being hardcoded per backend — this is the general pattern for extracting shared logic behind a `pub(super)` core type when two+ sibling modules are byte-for-byte identical except for one label.
- 2026-07-23: Every `src/tool/native/*` binary is a one-shot OS process per `tool_main.rs`: reads one JSON payload from stdin, prints one JSON result, exits — no persisted state, no task/session id passed in (confirmed via `src/host/executor/tool.rs`, which spawns each tool with bare stdin/stdout/stderr pipes only). Any "remember more than fits" feature (pagination, continuation) for these tools must therefore be stateless re-fetch/re-request-and-reslice on every call, matching the existing internal convention in `tool/native/process/output.rs` (`OutputSnapshot::from_path`: structured `offset`/`max_bytes` in, `next_offset`/`truncated` out, `offset > length` errors, `offset == length` is a valid empty read) — never a disk cache, and never a natural-language "call again with X" hint embedded in the content string.
- 2026-07-23: `web_fetch.rs` and `web_search.rs` (`src/tool/native/web/`) intentionally use two different error-helper styles that must each be preserved on their own file: `web_fetch.rs`'s `failure()` is a free module function (not `Self::`), so its own size-limit consts (`DEFAULT_MAX_LENGTH`/`MAX_MAX_LENGTH`) were added as free top-level `const`s (not inside `impl WebFetch`) specifically so error messages can use plain inline format captures like `{MAX_MAX_LENGTH}`; `web_search.rs`'s `failure()` is `Self::failure` and its `MAX_RESULTS` lives inside `impl WebSearch`, so its analogous message must use a non-inlined positional arg (`"...to {}", Self::MAX_RESULTS`) since `Self::X` path expressions cannot be inlined in a format string. Match whichever style a given file already uses rather than normalizing both to one convention.
- 2026-07-23: Wikipedia's `opensearch` API (`search_wikipedia` in `web_search.rs`) is prefix/relevance search, not count-based pagination — it has no `offset`/`s`/`b`-style parameter and always searches from the top regardless of caller-requested offset. When threading an `offset` through `WebSearch::search`'s provider fallback chain (DuckDuckGo → Yahoo → Wikipedia), only the first two providers gained the parameter; Wikipedia's call signature and behavior stayed untouched as a deliberate, documented limitation, not an oversight.

## Rust language gotchas hit during refactors

- When a private helper takes two-or-more reference-shaped parameters (e.g. `provider: &str` plus `value: Option<&serde_json::Value>`) and its return type borrows from only one of them, elision fails with E0106 ("missing lifetime specifier") because 2+ input lifetimes make the borrow source ambiguous. Fix by naming an explicit lifetime tied only to the parameter actually borrowed from, e.g. `fn f<'a>(provider: &str, value: Option<&'a Value>) -> Option<&'a [Value]>` — leave the unrelated parameter's lifetime elided/independent.
- If a `#[derive(Clone)]` struct gains a field whose type does not itself derive `Clone`, the derive fails at the *call site* (`Backend: Clone` bound not satisfied), not at the field's own definition — when wrapping shared state (e.g. `OpenAiCore`) inside multiple `Clone`-deriving wrapper structs, add `#[derive(Clone)]` to the shared type itself. `reqwest::Client` is `Arc`-backed internally, so cloning it is cheap and safe to do per-request/per-task.
- Clippy's `too_many_arguments` (default threshold 7) can appear as a *new* warning purely from splitting one bundled config struct into N free function parameters during a refactor, even when total information carried is unchanged. Prefer cloning the owning struct (`self.clone()`) into a single owned local and passing that one value into the spawned task/helper, over decomposing it into many scalar parameters — this is usually also a more faithful read of "use `self.field`"-style refactor instructions.
- The Rust coding style doc's ~500-line soft-file-size guideline can be explicitly overridden by an explicit task instruction to consolidate into "a single file, not a subdirectory" — when the two conflict, the explicit, more specific instruction wins; note the override rather than silently splitting the file back up.
- 2026-07-21: On this workspace's cargo version, `cargo fmt --check --workspace` errors ("unexpected argument '--workspace'" — it wants workspace args after `--`); use `cargo fmt --all --check` instead to format-check every workspace member.
- 2026-07-22: `clippy::large_enum_variant` (default ~200-byte threshold) can newly trigger purely from reshaping a variant from a tuple with one `InvocationSignature` field into a struct variant adding two more `String` fields — before concluding a clippy warning is "newly introduced by this change" rather than pre-existing, do a controlled A/B: temporarily swap back to the old shape, re-run clippy scoped to just the affected crate, and confirm the warning disappears. This codebase already carries several pre-existing, unaddressed instances of the same lint elsewhere (e.g. `SessionEvent` in `protocol/session/event.rs`, `StepEvent` in `protocol/step/event.rs`) without boxing large fields, so treat `large_enum_variant` as an accepted/tolerated lint class here — don't silently "fix" it by boxing fields when the enum shape itself was explicitly spec'd verbatim by the task.
- 2026-07-22: After swapping a file's content out-of-band via PowerShell (`Set-Content`/`Move-Item`) for a throwaway A/B clippy test and then restoring the original bytes, `cargo check`/`clippy` can report a suspiciously-fast "Finished" (around 1s) without actually recompiling the crate — Cargo's fingerprint check can fail to observe a real mtime change across the round-trip swap, so it silently reuses a stale cached artifact reflecting the *intermediate* (wrong) content, and the *next* `cargo check`/`clippy` invocation then surfaces spurious compile errors against what is actually correct, restored source. Fix: after any out-of-band file-swap test, force `(Get-Item path).LastWriteTime = Get-Date` and/or `cargo clean -p <pkg>` before trusting the next `cargo check`/`clippy` run — a single suspiciously-instant "Finished" is not proof of a genuine clean recompile.
- 2026-07-21: A "discriminator + N mandatory payload sections" config shape (e.g. `selected: String` plus always-required `deepseek: DeepseekConfig, glm: GlmConfig`) is strictly simpler to deserialize than a data-carrying enum: every section is a plain non-`Option` field, so a missing section surfaces for free as serde's own "missing field" error (bucketed under the existing `ConfigError::Toml` variant) — no custom `ConfigError` variant or manual validation needed. When replacing a data-carrying enum with this shape, `RawXxxx` intermediate-deserialization structs typically become unnecessary too: derive `Deserialize` directly on the real public struct, and reserve `#[serde(default = "...")]`/`#[serde(skip, default = "...")]` only for the few fields that are genuinely optional-with-a-computed-default (e.g. `core: CoreConfig` via `#[serde(default = "default_core_config")]`, `system: System` via `#[serde(skip, default = "System::new")]`) or need post-`toml::from_str` path resolution (e.g. `runtime`, `tool.directory`) — `build_config` then collapses to one `toml::from_str` call plus a couple of targeted field mutations, not a full manual struct reconstruction.
- 2026-07-21: A `grep` pattern using a long `|`-alternation group inside a quoted-string literal match (e.g. `"name": "(Config|RuntimeConfig|...|CONFIG_FILE)"` with 10+ alternatives) can return zero matches even though the content is present and a simpler single-term grep for one of the same alternatives succeeds immediately after — root cause not fully diagnosed (possibly an alternation-count limit or literal-parenthesis/quote escaping interaction). Workaround: prefer short, simple patterns (2-4 alternatives, or one distinctive substring) over long enumerated alternations when grepping JSON/text metadata.
- 2026-07-21: When a chain of two uncommitted tasks does "design A landed, then a follow-up task fully undoes/replaces design A with design B" without an intervening commit, `git diff` against the last real commit shows only the *net* structural delta — any type/field that was added by the first task and removed by the second never appears in the diff at all (as if it never existed). This is a good sanity check for design-metadata status decisions: if a `design.json` element was marked `added` (not `modified`/`renamed`) because "no prior evidence of it existing," a clean `git diff` against HEAD showing that same identifier as a fresh `+` addition (with no corresponding `-` removal of a same-named-but-different-shaped prior version) corroborates the call.

## Transport and configuration

- Remoc setup must keep its connection future driven after returning channels. Abort guarded connection tasks on timeout/rejection; otherwise failed handshakes leak workers.
- Server channel listeners bind the IPv4 wildcard independently of `server.ip`; connectors resolve the configured address. Server-driven host registration sends session identity and tool query before publishing the channel.
- Install a remote telemetry sink only after TCP, remoc, and token acceptance succeed. Startup telemetry exhaustion is fatal rather than a silent local fallback.

## Repository invariants

- Keep public protocol re-exports synchronized between `protocol/lib.rs` and its mirrored `protocol/mod.rs`.

## Logging and telemetry

- 2026-07-22: Logging severity is `LogLevel` ordered by declaration as `Debug < Info < Warning < Error`; telemetry queries treat `level` as a minimum and storage verifies `message.level >= query.level`.
- 2026-07-22: Telemetry redb schema v5 owns `telemetry_session_level_emit`, deletes the legacy exact-level index during transactional rebuild, and reads old primary JSON through `#[serde(alias = "tag")]` while all new serialization emits only `level`.
- 2026-07-22: `LogMessage.tags: Vec<String>` (`#[serde(default)]`, schema v6) is deduplicated in first-seen order via `LogMessage::dedup_tags` at construction time (`LogMessage::new`) — a single message's own tag list is NOT sorted. This is deliberately different from `Store::tags(session_id)`'s session-wide distinct-tag listing, which returns an alphabetically-sorted `Vec<String>` via `BTreeSet`. Don't conflate the two ordering conventions when touching either.
- 2026-07-22: Pattern for adding an opt-in parameter to heavily-called public methods without touching their signature (used for `Logger::log/warning/error/debug`, hundreds of existing call sites across the workspace): add sibling `_tagged` methods (`log_tagged`/`warning_tagged`/`error_tagged`/`debug_tagged`, each taking `tags: impl IntoIterator<Item = impl Into<String>>`) and thread the new data through the shared private impl only — `emit`/`telemetry` both grew a `tags: Vec<String>` parameter, with the original 4 public methods passing `Vec::new()` and the new 4 passing `Self::collect_tags(tags)`. Every existing call site keeps compiling untouched.
- 2026-07-22: New `SESSION_TAG_TABLE` redb table (`src/common/logging/store/schema.rs`) is keyed by `session_tag_key(session_id, tag) = session_key(session_id) ++ tag.as_bytes()`, one empty (`b""`) valued row per distinct (session, tag) pair, populated inside `index_message` (shared by `record_batch` and `rebuild_indexes`) via `for tag in &message.tags`. `Store::tags(session_id)` (new `store/tags.rs`) range-scans just that session's key prefix, strips the prefix to recover each tag string, and collects into a `BTreeSet<String>` for free dedup+sort — this needed a `SCHEMA_VERSION` bump (5→6) so `ensure_schema()`'s `required`-table check forces old stores to `rebuild_indexes()` and backfill it. By contrast, tag *filtering* needed neither a new table nor a schema bump: `Store::matches(message, query, keyword)` in `reader.rs` just grew one more `&&`-chained OR-check (`query.tags.is_empty() || query.tags.iter().any(|tag| message.tags.contains(tag))`) appended after the existing level/keyword checks — since every scan path (`index_range_records`, `trigram_index_records`, `session_incremental_records`) already funnels through this one shared predicate, the new filter applies everywhere from a single edit site.
- 2026-07-22: `Logger::log_tagged`/`error_tagged` call sites take their tag list as an array literal (`["Model Relay"]`); growing it to N tags is a pure literal edit (`["Model Relay", "Request"]`) with zero syntax adjustment needed — a `[&'static str; N]` array satisfies `tags: impl IntoIterator<Item = impl Into<String>>` for any N, confirmed in `src/server/model/openai.rs`'s 4 call sites (moving `[Request]`/`[Response]` out of the message-text bracket and into a second tag, while `[{task_id}]` stayed in the text since task IDs are unbounded-cardinality and unsuited to the low-cardinality Tags dropdown).
- 2026-07-22: After a text-only shortening edit to a `Logger::*_tagged` call's `format!(...)` message argument, `cargo fmt` can leave one call site inline but reflow a **textually near-identical sibling** call site (same arg shapes/arity, similar string lengths, well under the 100-char `max_width`) into 4-line block form instead — the split is not simply "does the single-line version exceed max_width" (verified by measuring the hypothetical single-line reconstruction at 90 chars, under 100, and even reproducing a minimal standalone `rustfmt` snippet with the same shape that stayed inline). Cause not fully isolated; don't hand-guess/pre-format call sites after this kind of edit — just run `cargo fmt -p <crate>` and trust `cargo fmt --check -p <crate>` (must exit clean) as the only ground truth.

## Design-metadata (`src_meta/`) nuances

- 2026-07-21: `pub(super)` counts as "outward-facing" for `design-json-update` element-tracking purposes, same as `pub`/`pub(crate)`/`pub(in ...)` — a `pub(super) fn` gets its own tracked element even though it never appears in the crate's genuine public API surface.
- 2026-07-21: When a private function is renamed and the *old* name was never itself tracked as a design.json element (e.g. it only became `pub(super)`/tracked-worthy as part of the same change that renamed it), classify it as a net-new `added` element at its new name — not a `renamed` element — because there is no prior element identity to rename in place. Reserve `renamed`/rename-in-place strictly for identifiers that already had their own tracked element under the old name.
- 2026-07-21: `src/common/structure/tests/channel.rs` (any path under a `tests/` folder, at any depth) gets zero design.json treatment — it is not scanned for elements and its changes never appear in any folder's status arrays, confirmed via both the `changed`- and `full`-mode path rules.
- 2026-07-21: Files/folders directly under `src/common/structure/` are covered by two design.json layers at once: `src_meta/common/structure/design.json` (native depth) and, one level up, `src_meta/common/design.json` — the latter carries a mirrored copy of the same `structure`-owned elements one layer down (per the "elements = own layer + direct child modules one layer down" rule), so both files must be edited together and kept in sync whenever a `structure/*.rs` element set changes. `src_meta/design.json` (repo root) only needs `modified: ["."]` plus purpose/child-summary wording when the change is a pure descendant with no direct file under `src/` itself.
- 2026-07-22: A task prompt asked this agent to itself recompute/correct `codeSegments` line ranges in `src_meta/server/model/design.json` after a `cargo fmt` reflow shifted line numbers in `src/server/model/openai.rs` (framed as "prior turns did this same fix"), but per this agent's own scope this agent never edits anything under `src_meta/` — that boundary held even though the request was phrased as a settled precedent. Findings were reported instead of applied: `OpenAiCore`'s 3rd codeSegment (`lineStart:71, lineEnd:168`, `modifiedLines:[{166,166}]`) needs its end and the modified-line span extended by the net insertion (log_response's call went from 1 line to 4, so `168→171` and `{166,166}→{166,169}`), and the 4th segment (`lineStart:170, lineEnd:242`, all `addedLines`) needs both bounds shifted +3 (`170→173`, `242→245`) — mirrored identically in `src_meta/server/design.json` (same element, byte-for-byte duplicate block). Also noticed pre-existing, unrelated-to-this-edit bad data on this same element: a 5th codeSegment claims `lineStart:700, lineEnd:709` in `openai.rs`, but the file only has 673 lines total (670 before this session's edit) — that range was already out-of-bounds before today's change and is not something this agent introduced or corrected.
- 2026-07-21: The "own layer + direct child module one layer down" mirroring rule is applied inconsistently pre-existing across the repo — confirmed by directly comparing three real files during one `config.rs` rewrite: `src_meta/common/config/design.json` (own-layer, depth-3) tracks public structs/enums plus `CONFIG_FILE` but *not* private free functions or the other private statics (`CONFIG_ENV_VAR`, `CONFIG_CACHE`); `src_meta/common/design.json` (mirror one level up) tracks the *same* structs/enums/`CONFIG_FILE` **plus** every private free function and both other private statics; `src_meta/server/design.json` (one level above `src/server/relay`) mirrors **nothing** from `relay`'s elements at all — just a `childModules` purpose-summary entry, no element list. When a change touches a deeply-nested file, always check the immediate-parent design.json's *existing* convention for that specific child (does it mirror elements at all? functions too, or just structs/enums?) rather than assuming one of the three patterns — do not try to harmonize them, per the skill's own explicit warning that conventions are inconsistent.
- 2026-07-21: An embedded test-fixture TOML string that lives inside a `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` block *within* an ordinary source file (e.g. `src/server_telemetry/server.rs`'s fixture, not a separate `tests/`-folder file) is not literally covered by the skill's "ignore folders named tests" rule, since it's not a folder — but the established repo precedent (confirmed by checking that an equivalent prior-task edit to the same fixture left that file's design.json completely untouched, `changeStatus: "unchanged"`, no top-level `modified` array at all) is to treat it the same way: skip design.json edits entirely for changes confined to an in-file `#[cfg(test)]` module, since test code isn't itself a tracked source element and the containing function's own `codeSegments` range doesn't extend into the test module.
- 2026-07-21: Promoting a dependency already resolved transitively (e.g. `socket2` locked at `0.6.4` via `tokio`) to a direct dependency at a compatible version constraint produces a minimal `Cargo.lock` diff — just one line added to the crate's own dependency list, no version/checksum churn elsewhere. Not a sign something is wrong; expected and cheap to verify with `git diff -- src/Cargo.lock`.
- 2026-07-21: `cargo fmt` will reflow a hand-written multi-line `.map_err(|e| ...)` closure or a multi-arg logging call into whatever line count it prefers (sometimes collapsing, sometimes expanding) — always run `cargo fmt -p <crate>` and re-`--check` after hand-editing non-trivial expressions from task prose rather than trusting the pasted formatting verbatim.
- Logging from deep inside `src/common` submodules (e.g. `structure/channel.rs`) uses `use crate::Logger;` (re-exported at the crate root), the same convention already used by `src/common/actor/actor.rs` and `runtime.rs` — check crate-root re-exports before assuming a fully-qualified module path is needed.
- 2026-07-22: A struct can be declared in a parent file while most of its `impl` blocks live in sibling files pulled in via `#[path=...]` mod declarations — concretely `Store` is declared in `src/common/logging/store.rs` (which also declares `mod schema; mod reader; mod tags; mod writer;`), but its methods live across `store/schema.rs`, `store/reader.rs`, and `store/tags.rs`. The design.json element is tracked at the *declaring* file's module layer (`logging/design.json`, not `store/design.json`), with one `codeSegments` array spanning all those sibling files. Check where the `struct`/`enum` keyword itself lives, never where most of the behavior lives.
- 2026-07-22: Confirmed concretely (via grep across all 51 `design.json` files in the repo) that the "own layer + direct child module one layer down" mirroring rule hard-stops at 2 layers with no exceptions: a `src/server/model/openai.rs` change (`OpenAiCore`) propagates to `server/model/design.json` and `server/design.json` only — it never appears in root `src_meta/design.json`, no matter how many ancestor folders exist above `model/`. Never propagate a deeply-nested file's element changes past its own folder plus that folder's immediate parent.
- 2026-07-22: A "pure text-shortening inside an existing `format!()` string, zero lines added/removed" edit is NOT guaranteed to leave line counts unchanged once `cargo fmt --check` is satisfied: shortening `Logger::error_tagged(format!("[Model Relay][{task_id}][Request] {error}"), ["Model Relay"])` from 4 lines down to under rustfmt's width limit made `cargo fmt` collapse it to 1 line — across 4 call sites in one file (`src/server/model/openai.rs`) this removed 12 lines total and shifted every element/impl-block boundary after the first edit site. Always re-derive exact current line numbers (grep for the element's opening/closing braces plus the specific changed lines) from the *post-`cargo fmt`* source, never from the pre-edit view, before touching `codeSegments` ranges in the module's own `design.json` and its one-layer-up mirror — both must be recomputed together (confirmed via the `design-json-update` skill, mode `changed`, driven by a `.temp/changed/<turn>.txt` marker) since both carry identical copies of the same element's segments.
- 2026-07-22: A private helper used by exactly one public function can be tracked as a second `codeSegments` entry bundled onto that public function's element rather than getting its own element — existing precedent: `parse_level` (private, `server_telemetry/handlers.rs`) is bundled onto `logs`'s element as its second segment. Preserve this bundling precedent where it already exists rather than splitting it into a separate element.
- 2026-07-22: Without git access, reconstruct pre-edit line-shapes via arithmetic: if N call sites are each claimed to transform the same mechanical way (e.g. "N single-line `Logger::log(...)` calls become N 4-line `log_tagged(...)` calls"), check whether the containing block's total observed line growth equals `N × (new-lines-per-call − old-lines-per-call)` for that shape — an exact match strongly corroborates the added/modified/pure-shift split without ever diffing against a prior commit.
- 2026-07-22: Confirmed concretely (via grep for the exact test fn names across all of `src_meta/`) that deleting two `#[test] fn` blocks from `message.rs`'s `mod tests` needed zero element-status changes — neither test name appeared anywhere in `design.json`, corroborating the line-57 rule that in-file `#[cfg(test)]` modules are never tracked. But it still required routine line-range housekeeping in both `src_meta/common/logging/design.json` and its mirror `src_meta/common/design.json`: `LogMessage`'s third `codeSegments` entry (the private `impl` block holding `dedup_tags`/`now_ms`, physically *after* `mod tests` in the file) had to shift its `lineStart`/`lineEnd`/`addedLines` upward by exactly the 20 removed lines (102→82, 117→97, 103-109→83-89) in both files identically, since that segment's bounds are absolute source line numbers and the deletion was upstream of it. General rule: after removing lines from a tracked file, always recheck every sibling `codeSegments` entry for that same file whose range starts *after* the deleted region, in every `design.json` that mirrors it — not just the ones for elements that "look related."
- 2026-07-22: **Validation-checklist blind spot**: the skill's own Validation steps catch only *structural* problems (JSON parses, required fields present, ranges in-bounds) — they cannot catch a codeSegment that's simply missing `modifiedLines` for a real change. Concretely, `Logger`'s public-impl segment initially had correct `addedLines` for 4 brand-new `_tagged` methods but an empty `modifiedLines`, silently missing that the 4 pre-existing `log/warning/error/debug` methods each had their `emit(...)` call site's argument list changed (one new trailing `Vec::new()` arg per call). This under-highlighting shape — a big new contiguous block correctly caught, several small scattered one-line pre-existing-call-site edits nearby missed — reproduces easily whenever N old call sites each gain "one more argument." Always re-read the live source line-by-line against recorded highlights for such elements before calling a design.json update done; the structural validation script alone is not sufficient evidence of correctness.
- 2026-07-22: The line-66 shift rule generalizes down to the smallest possible deletion: removing a single `assert!(message.tags.is_empty());` line from inside `message.rs`'s `mod tests` (one line, not a whole `#[test] fn`) again needed zero element-status change (test body isn't tracked) but still required the exact same downstream housekeeping — `LogMessage`'s third `codeSegments` entry (the private `impl` block after `mod tests`) shifted by exactly 1 line in both `src_meta/common/logging/design.json` and its mirror: 82→81, 97→96, and the nested `dedup_tags` `addedLines` highlight 83-89→82-88. Confirms the rule is deletion-size-agnostic: any N-line removal upstream of a segment shifts that segment's bounds by exactly N, down to N=1, with no lower bound where the housekeeping becomes optional.
- 2026-07-22: Deleting an entire `#[cfg(test)] mod tests { ... }` block outright (whole module, not just individual fns/lines inside it, per an explicit user instruction to remove test coverage rather than relocate it — done for `src/client/cli/main.rs`, `src/server_telemetry/server.rs`, and `src/common/logging/message.rs` in one turn) confirms both halves of the line-57/66/68 rule family at once: (1) `client/cli/main.rs`'s and `server_telemetry/server.rs`'s test modules were each the *last* thing in the file (EOF right after the closing `}`), and in both cases the pre-existing tracked element's own `codeSegments` (the `marix-client-cli` bin in `src_meta/client/cli/design.json` + its mirror `src_meta/client/design.json`; the `serve` fn in `src_meta/server_telemetry/design.json` + its mirror root `src_meta/design.json`) already ended exactly at the last production line *before* the test module (`main.rs` lineEnd 270, `server.rs` lineEnd 50) — confirming whoever tracked them originally already excluded the test module from the range, so a whole-module EOF deletion needs **zero** codeSegments edits in either the folder's own design.json or its one-layer-up mirror. (2) `message.rs`'s test module sat *mid-file* (private `impl LogMessage` for `dedup_tags`/`now_ms` follows it, separated by a `// -- Private -- //` marker), so deleting all 25 lines from the blank separator through the module's closing `}` (`#[cfg(test)]` at old line 54 through `}` at old line 77, plus the redundant leading blank line 53) required the now-familiar shift, this time by -25: `LogMessage`'s third `codeSegments` entry moved 81→56 / 96→71, `addedLines` 82-88→57-63, identically in both `src_meta/common/logging/design.json` and `src_meta/common/design.json`. When collapsing a leading blank-line-plus-module deletion into one `edit`/line-removal, keep exactly one blank line as the surviving separator (delete the *leading* blank, keep the *trailing* one already present before the next item) so `cargo fmt --check` doesn't trip on a doubled or EOF-trailing blank line — verified clean afterward with `cargo fmt --all --check` (note: `cargo fmt --check --workspace` is not valid syntax for `cargo-fmt`; `--workspace` is a `cargo` build-graph flag the `fmt` subcommand doesn't accept — use `cargo fmt --all --check` instead, `cargo fmt` is inherently workspace/all-local-packages-scoped when `--all` is passed).
